perturbation.original_text: Operator shall provide Owner within 10 days of receipt of such Liability Claim a copy of any demand letter, petition, or similar documentation of the Liability Claim.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator shall provide Owner within 30 days of receipt of such Liability Claim a copy of any demand letter, petition, or similar documentation of the Liability Claim.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notification period from 10 to 30 days for liability claims may impede Owner's ability to promptly investigate and respond to claims within the timeframe required by Louisiana law. This creates a legal contradiction by potentially hindering Owner's compliance with state regulations regarding timely responses to legal claims.
perturbation.law_explanation: Louisiana Revised Statutes 22:1220 imposes a duty of good faith and fair dealing on insurers, which includes promptly investigating and responding to claims. By extending the notification period to 30 days, the operator could hinder the owner's ability to comply with these requirements, potentially leading to violations of Louisiana insurance regulations and exposing them to penalties.

perturbation.justification: The change from 10 to 30 days introduces a potential inconsistency with the duty of good faith and fair dealing imposed on insurers under Louisiana law, as explained by the LLM Law Explanation. Although Scraped Law Snippet 2 indicates the statute (22:1220) has been redesignated, the LLM explanation still connects the extended notification period to a possible hindrance of the owner's ability to promptly investigate and respond to claims, potentially violating Louisiana insurance regulations. This extended timeframe could impede the owner's ability to meet the promptness requirements associated with good faith claims handling, creating an inconsistency that undermines the legal standard, thus falling under Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition. The perturbation explanation states that this change may impede owner's ability to promptly investigate and respond to claims within the timeframe required by Louisiana law.

perturbation.original_text: Such evaluation and modification, if necessary, of the Containment Pan System shall occur within ninety (90) days of the Effective Date.
perturbation.changed_text: Such evaluation and modification, if necessary, of the Containment Pan System shall occur within one hundred eighty (180) days of the Effective Date.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the timeline for containment system evaluation and modification to 180 days may conflict with the EPA's SPCC rule requirements for prompt action to prevent oil spills. This delay could result in non-compliance and potential fines under federal environmental regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 40 CFR § 112.7(a) requires facilities to implement spill prevention measures as soon as possible. Delaying the evaluation and modification of the containment system to 180 days could be deemed as failing to implement required preventative measures promptly, thus violating the SPCC rule.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) for Agriculture | US EPA Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. JavaScript appears to be disabled on this computer. Please click here to see any active alerts . Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) for Agriculture Oil spills endanger public health, impact drinking water, devastate natural resources, and disrupt the economy. Every effort must be made to prevent oil spills and to clean them up promptly once they occur. The purpose of the Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure (SPCC) rule is to help facilities and farms prevent a discharge of oil into navigable waters or adjoining shorelines. The SPCC rule requires farms and other facilities to develop, maintain, and implement an oil spill prevention plan, called an SPCC Plan. These plans help farms prevent oil spills, as well as control a spill should one occur. If you have additional questions about the SPCC program, please call our Oil Information Center at (800) 424-9346. Updates - Water Resources Reform and Development Act (WRRDA) SPCC Basics Create Your SPCC Plan Additional Information and Resources Updates Study Required by the Water Resources Reform and Development Act (WRRDA) and Future Rulemaking WRRDA directed EPA to conduct a study to determine the appropriate applicability threshold for farms, based on a significant risk of discharge to water. EPA consulted with the U.S. Department of Agriculture to gather the most recent and complete information about the characteristics of farms, particularly with regard to oil storage. EPA expects to promulgate a rule amending the SPCC requirements associated with the applicability thresholds and other WRRDA amendments. Oil Storage on U.S. Farms: Risks and Opportunities for Protecting Surface Waters Farms and WRRDA WRRDA was signed into law by the President on June 10, 2014. Section 1049 of the Act changes certain applicability provisions of the SPCC rule for farms, and modifies the criteria under which a farmer may self-certify an SPCC Plan. New Farms Fact Sheet Explaining the Impact of the WRRDA on the SPCC rule New Farms Fact Sheet SPCC Basics A Facility Owner/Operator’s Guide to Oil Pollution Prevention SPCC Farms Fact Sheet provides a brief overview of the SPCC program as it relates to farms. Note: This SPCC fact sheet is currently out of date. Please see the new WRRDA fact sheet for updated information on changes to farm SPCC applicability and the self-certification criteria. Fact Sheet: Oil Discharge Reporting Requirements Fact Sheet: SPCC Compliance Date Extension for Farms How to Fill Out a Tier I Template for Farms Frequent SPCC Questions for Farms Create Your SPCC Plan A number of resources are available to help farms create their SPCC Plan. The Tier I Template is intended to help the owner or operator of a Tier I qualified facility develop a self-certified SPCC Plan. See also: Is My Facility a "Qualified Facility" under the SPCC Rule? Example Tier I Qualified Facility SPCC Plan - this example plan, based on a farm scenario, will guide you through creating your own self-certified SPCC Plan. How to Fill Out a Tier I Template - this presentation walks you through how to fill out a Tier I Template using the example Tier I Qualified Facility SPCC Plan above. Secondary Containment Calculation Worksheets - example and blank worksheets used to calculate secondary containment capacity are available to help you to comply with the secondary containment requirements of the SPCC rule. mySPCC - a suite of compliance assistance tools has been specifically developed to provide agricultural retailers with industry-standard information to assist in the preparation of an SPCC Plan for
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the timeline for evaluation and modification of the containment system to 180 days, which contradicts the requirement for prompt action to prevent oil spills, as stated in the provided law explanation and supported by the EPA's focus on preventing oil spills and cleaning them up promptly, as mentioned in Scraped Law Snippet 2. The original text provided 90 days, while the changed text extends it to 180 days. The law explanation references 40 CFR § 112.7(a), which requires facilities to implement spill prevention measures as soon as possible, and the modified contract delays these measures, increasing the risk of non-compliance. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where time-sensitive obligations violate federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: Owner shall approve or reject the proposed Capital Project set forth in the applicable Capital Project Proposal within fifteen (15) days from the date of receipt of the same, such approval not to be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed.
perturbation.changed_text: Owner shall approve or reject the proposed Capital Project set forth in the applicable Capital Project Proposal within forty-five (45) days from the date of receipt of the same, such approval not to be unreasonably withheld, conditioned or delayed.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the approval period for capital projects to 45 days could potentially delay necessary environmental upgrades, conflicting with regulations that require timely implementation of environmental protection measures. Specifically, if a capital project is necessary to address a known environmental hazard or compliance issue at the Rail Facility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA) regulations, specifically those related to corrective action, 40 CFR § 264.100
perturbation.law_explanation: RCRA regulations, particularly 40 CFR § 264.100, mandate prompt corrective action for releases of hazardous waste or constituents. If a Capital Project is required to address such a release, a 45-day approval period might be deemed unreasonably long, especially if it delays the start of essential environmental remediation work, leading to non-compliance with RCRA.

perturbation.justification: The modification extends the approval period for capital projects from 15 to 45 days, potentially delaying environmental upgrades. The LLM explanation states that RCRA regulations, specifically 40 CFR § 264.100, require prompt corrective action for hazardous waste releases. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which references eCFR :: 40 CFR 264.100, confirms the existence of a corrective action program under RCRA. If a Capital Project is required to address such a release, a 45-day approval period could be considered unreasonably long, leading to non-compliance, and therefore creating a legal contradiction under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.
